---
title: "NCOS Mesa Vegetation StoryMap: Structure Outline"
author: "Lukas Lescano, Garrett Mellinger, Minh Tri Ngo"
date: "2026-05-18"
format: pdf
---
# StoryMap Outline: Effects of 2023 Burn and 2024 Grazing on NCOS Mesa Grass Cover

# Research Questions and Variables

**Overarching question:**
- How does the percent cover of native grasses vary over time compared to the percent cover of exotic vegetation on the NCOS Mesa (Perennial Grasslands)?

**Sub-questions:**
- How does intentional disturbance (2023 burn, 2024 grazing) influence this relationship?
- How do key individual species (Stipa pulchra, Medicago polymorpha, Melilotus indicus, Festuca perennis) vary in percent cover before and after intentional disturbance?

**Response variables:**
- Native grass percent cover
- Non-native species percent cover
- Stipa pulchra percent cover
- Melilotus indicus percent cover
- Medicago polymorpha percent cover
- Festuca perennis percent cover

## Section 1: Site Context
- Cover image + title block
- Location map of NCOS Mesa (static or lightly interactive, NOT the dashboard)
- What the Mesa is and why Perennial Grassland matters
- Scope statement: GL-prefix transects only, GL-11 excluded (unburned), 7 transects total
- Brief intro to the Garrett's NCOS field connection (ground-truth credibility)

## Section 2: The Treatments and the Question
- Timeline graphic: surveys 2021-2025, burn 2023, grazing 2024
  - 2023 = pre-burn, 2024 = post-burn, 2025 = post-grazing
- State the overarching question plainly: native vs. non-native cover over time
- Introduce the disturbance sub-question: why burn, then graze (restoration goal: favor native perennial grasses)
- Mostly narrative / text, possibly a sidecar block

## Section 3: What We Found (Figure Walkthrough)
- Guided sequence; maps onto the question hierarchy, sets up spatial patterns before the dashboard

- Answers the OVERARCHING question (native vs. non-native over time):
  - Figure 1: native vs. non-native grass time series
    - Native peaks 2023 pre-burn, then declines; both converge and decline post-grazing

- Answers SUB-QUESTION 2 (individual species before/after disturbance):
  - Figure 4: clover time series (M. indicus, M. polymorpha)
    - Both spike post-burn; bur clover peaks 2024 then declines, sweet clover keeps rising
    - Note for group: unexplained 2022 dip to near zero
  - Figure 3: diverging bar chart, percent point change for 4 focal species
  - Statistics callout: paired t-test results
    - S. pulchra down (p=0.003), M. indicus up (p=0.013), F. perennis ns (p=0.350), M. polymorpha ns (p=0.716)

- Note: SUB-QUESTION 1 (does disturbance influence the relationship) is answered across all three figures via the burn/grazing markers, not a separate figure

## Section 4: Interactive Exploration (Embedded Dashboard)
- Reader is now primed by the figures to read spatial patterns
- Embedded ArcGIS Dashboard
  - Layer 1: photo points (popups with field photos per transect)
  - Layer 2: percent-cover heatmap (interpolated; methodology TBD)
  - Interaction scope: click photo points, toggle heatmap [CONFIRM with An: enough to justify Dashboard vs. simpler embedded web map?]
  - Possible addition: year selector / time slider so heatmap shows change across 2021-2025 (renders the disturbance sub-question spatially)
- Purpose: let readers ground-truth the figures and see spatial structure transects can't show
- Heatmap caveat placed HERE, near the map, not buried in limitations

## Section 5: Limitations and Next Steps
- Double-mean aggregation in transect-point layer (Figure 1)
- No multiple-comparison correction on t-tests (flag: ask An)
- Interpolation from 7 linear transects = illustrative, not predictive
- 2022 clover dip needs explanation
- Future: refine interpolation methodology, expand temporal coverage

## Open Questions for Group / Professor
- Dashboard interactivity: is photos + heatmap toggle enough, or add filters (by year? by transect?)
- Heatmap interpolation method (IDW vs. kriging) and how to communicate uncertainty
- Whether to add a year-selector so the heatmap animates burn -> grazing change
